games.explanation: Bradish has been excellent in a small sample, but the platoon split inside that line is the whole story: he's been a different pitcher against lefties, and Washington happens to run a lineup full of them. Wood, García, and Abrams all swing from the left side, and the splits show Bradish has been walking lefties at a double-digit clip while the hard contact piles up. Littell isn't going to overpower Alonso or Henderson, but a homer-friendly park with a soft cross-breeze cuts both ways, and the sim has this as essentially a coin flip. At +168 on a team the market has buried as a two-to-one dog, you're getting paid like Bradish's righty numbers apply to everyone in the box.

Bieber's headline line looks tidy until you split it by handedness, and Texas happens to run out a lineup full of the exact bats that have been tattooing him. Righties have posted a .404 xwOBA against him this year, which is a polite way of saying he has no answer for them, and Seager in particular is the kind of right-on-right monster who turns command mistakes into damage. Rocker walks too many people to be anyone's idea of an ace, but he doesn't have to be tonight, he just has to keep pace with a starter the board is still pricing on reputation. Getting plus money on the better team, in a roof game, against a pitcher whose split sheet is screaming, is the whole story.

Tampa is being priced like the obvious side here, but Rasmussen has quietly been more hittable than his ERA suggests and Arizona happens to bring the wrong kind of lineup to find out. Marte, Carroll, and Perdomo are all running xwOBAs north of .350, and Rasmussen's stuff plays down against lefty bats who don't chase. Merrill Kelly isn't an ace but he's stable enough to keep this to the kind of one-run game the board insists it won't be. At +162 on a coin flip our numbers say is closer to even, you take the price and let the math work.

The market sees a Cardinals home game and a Marlins logo and stops reading, which is how you end up with plus money on the better team. Miami is the stronger club by a wide Elo margin tonight, and Kyle Leahy is the kind of contact-prone righty who hands free baserunners to lefty bats, with a walk rate north of 11% against them. That plays directly into a Marlins order stacked with Stowers, Edwards, and Hernández, while Tyler Phillips quietly runs a barrel rate against that most aces would sign for. At +114, you are getting the favorite priced like the dog.

Atlanta is the home team and the recognizable name on the marquee, which is the only reason the Giants are getting this kind of price in a coin-flip game. Sale is the headline arm, but he's a lefty starter facing a San Francisco lineup that quietly stacks right-handed thump (Chapman, Schmitt, and a Devers who's been mashing right-handers to the tune of a .391 xwOBA) and happens to be hitting in the most run-suppressing park on the road map. Ray isn't the equal of Sale on paper, but he doesn't have to be in a venue that turns barrels into outs and walks into wasted baserunners. The board has this game priced like a mismatch when the projection lands inside a tenth of a run, and +137 on the actual coin flip is the entire pitch.

picks.explanation: Bradish has been excellent in a small sample, but the platoon split inside that line is the whole story: he's been a different pitcher against lefties, and Washington happens to run a lineup full of them. Wood, García, and Abrams all swing from the left side, and the splits show Bradish has been walking lefties at a double-digit clip while the hard contact piles up. Littell isn't going to overpower Alonso or Henderson, but a homer-friendly park with a soft cross-breeze cuts both ways, and the sim has this as essentially a coin flip. At +168 on a team the market has buried as a two-to-one dog, you're getting paid like Bradish's righty numbers apply to everyone in the box.

Baltimore is being priced like a team that's going to roll, and the runline is begging you to remember that Washington has a lineup full of left-handed bats and Kyle Bradish has spent his small sample getting tagged by exactly that profile. Bradish's numbers against righties look untouchable, but flip the card to lefties and the walk rate balloons past 12% with a hard-hit rate over 42, which is the door Wood and Abrams walk through. Littell isn't a strikeout guy, but he throws strikes in a park that plays bigger than its reputation suggests once the wind blows across, and a one-score game is exactly what the sim keeps spitting out. Getting plus the run at -125 on a coin-flip projection is the whole reason you log in.

Bieber's headline line looks tidy until you split it by handedness, and Texas happens to run out a lineup full of the exact bats that have been tattooing him. Righties have posted a .404 xwOBA against him this year, which is a polite way of saying he has no answer for them, and Seager in particular is the kind of right-on-right monster who turns command mistakes into damage. Rocker walks too many people to be anyone's idea of an ace, but he doesn't have to be tonight, he just has to keep pace with a starter the board is still pricing on reputation. Tampa is being priced like the obvious side here, but Rasmussen has quietly been more hittable than his ERA suggests and Arizona happens to bring the wrong kind of lineup to find out. Marte, Carroll, and Perdomo are all running xwOBAs north of .350, and Rasmussen's stuff plays down against lefty bats who don't chase. Merrill Kelly isn't an ace but he's stable enough to keep this to the kind of one-run game the board insists it won't be. At +162 on a coin flip our numbers say is closer to even, you take the price and let the math work.

Tampa is the better team here, nobody is arguing that, but they're not the kind of better that should be laying a run and a half against a Diamondbacks lineup that quietly hits everyone. The top of Arizona's order is a problem for any righty, and Marte in particular has been mashing right-handed pitching all year with a .385 xwOBA that suggests Rasmussen's tidy ratios are about to get tested. Kelly isn't an ace but he keeps games close, and a projected one-run margin doesn't square with a market that's pricing this like a comfortable Rays win. Take the cushion at -135 and let the back end of the bullpen sort itself out.

The market sees a Cardinals home game and a Marlins logo and stops reading, which is how you end up with plus money on the better team. Miami is the stronger club by a wide Elo margin tonight, and Kyle Leahy is the kind of contact-prone righty who hands free baserunners to lefty bats, with a walk rate north of 11% against them. That plays directly into a Marlins order stacked with Stowers, Edwards, and Hernández, while Tyler Phillips quietly runs a barrel rate against that most aces would sign for. At +114, you are getting the favorite priced like the dog.

Atlanta is the home team and the recognizable name on the marquee, which is the only reason the Giants are getting this kind of price in a coin-flip game. Sale is the headline arm, but he's a lefty starter facing a San Francisco lineup that quietly stacks right-handed thump (Chapman, Schmitt, and a Devers who's been mashing right-handers to the tune of a .391 xwOBA) and happens to be hitting in the most run-suppressing park on the road map. Ray isn't the equal of Sale on paper, but he doesn't have to be in a venue that turns barrels into outs and walks into wasted baserunners. The board has this game priced like a mismatch when the projection lands inside a tenth of a run, and +137 on the actual coin flip is the entire pitch.

Chris Sale against a Giants lineup that runs four lefty-neutralizing bats through the top is not the buzzsaw the price implies, and a run and a half of cushion in a pitcher's park is the kind of insurance the market keeps forgetting to charge for. Devers in particular has been a different hitter against righties, posting a .391 xwOBA that Sale's lefty splits do nothing to suppress, and Chapman gives them a second bat that does damage on contact regardless of the arm angle. Truist is playing small tonight, with a runs factor that knocks nearly a tenth off the scoreboard, and the projection lands on a one-run game where the Giants only need to lose by exactly one to cash. Ray is wild enough to keep this messy, which is fine, because messy games are where +1.5 lives. Paying -125 for the better side of a coin flip with a free run attached is the entire pitch.
